issue: Strengthening the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons review process

entries.position_summary: The United States acknowledges the NPT’s strong record but notes the review process has been uneven, sometimes fostering division. It supports improving and strengthening the review process to better serve and unite the regime.
entries.quote: Statement by the United States to the NPT Preparatory Committee Cluster Three Specific Issue: Strengthened Review Process Statement as delivered by Mark Goodman, Senior Scientist, Office of Multilateral Nuclear and Security Affairs, Bureau of International Security and Nonproliferation, U.S. Department of State Geneva, Switzerland July 30, 2024 Thank you Chair, The Non-Proliferation Treaty has served us well for more than five decades, making inestimable contributions to international security and development. Its record is undeniable. Sadly, however, the NPT review process has more of a mixed record, sometimes bringing us together to strengthen the NPT regime, sometimes highlighting divisions and stoking our frustration. I think we all agree that it could work better.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports strengthening the NPT review process, submitting proposals outlining improvements and endorsing the Chair’s draft recommendations and compromise measures. It believes these have broad support and provide a solid basis for consensus.
entries.quote: 1. The United States submitted a working paper1 to the meeting of the working group on further strengthening the review process of the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons, held on 24 to 28 July 2023, which identified several areas in which the review process could be improved and laying out options and issues for consideration by States parties to the Non-Proliferation Treaty. The present working paper provides an update based on the draft recommendations of the Chair (NPT/CONF.2026/WG.I/CRP.2/Rev.1), which the Chair later submitted as a working paper to the first session (held in 2023) of the Preparatory Committee for the 2026 Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (NPT/CONF.2026/PC.I/WP.34) and on the deliberations of the working group, including the compromise proposal submitted by four States at the end of the working group’s deliberations. Collectively, these proposals had broad support and provide a solid basis for reaching consensus on measures to strengthen the Non-Proliferation Treaty review process.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports reforms to strengthen and streamline the NPT review process—reducing duplication across meetings, enforcing time limits in plenaries, and enabling interactive discussions of national reports—but regrets that some States Parties blocked consensus on the chair’s draft decision. It is ready to support these measures, provided other nuclear-weapon states do likewise.
entries.quote: Finally, Chair, we regret the fact that a few States Parties were unwilling to work towards consensus on your draft decision on strengthening the review process, which the United States was ready to support. In a time of constrained resources, the United States has made clear our position on the need to reform multilateral institutions to be more effective and efficient. We continue to support good ideas, such as proposals for reducing duplication across meetings, for adhering to strict time limits during plenary sessions, and for interactive discussion of national reports, so long as all other nuclear-weapon States do the same. We deeply regret, despite the best efforts of many of us to get this done, not all were willing to do so.

entries.position_summary: The country supports establishing a working group to strengthen the NPT review process, noting broad member support despite differing substantive priorities. It welcomes the chair’s elements paper circulated during the PrepCom.
entries.quote: to hold a working group aimed at improving the review process. Over the course of this review cycle, Member States have clearly articulated the need for strengthening the review process, regardless of differences in our substantive priorities. We appreciate that the chair has drafted and circulated an elements paper on this important topic during this PrepCom.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates improving the effectiveness of the NPT review process by enhancing the Preparatory Committee’s role in setting the agenda for the Review Conference. This indicates support for a more structured and outcome-oriented review cycle.
entries.quote: • improving our use of the Preparatory Committee to set the agenda for the Review Conference; and

entries.position_summary: The country advocates strengthening the NPT review process to build trust and credibility and to ensure coordinated, continuous efforts across all three pillars. It calls for seizing the current opportunity to shape the future of the review process.
entries.quote: Chair, Much work has already gone into efforts to strengthen the review process. We all have a special opportunity to shape the future of this review process that builds trust and credibility and ensures coordinated and continuous efforts across all three pillars. Let us seize this moment.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports reforms to make the NPT review process more effective and efficient, including reducing duplication across meetings, enforcing strict plenary time limits, and enabling interactive discussion of national reports. It is willing to back good ideas if all Nuclear-Weapon States also commit to these improvements.
entries.quote: Finally, the United States has made clear the need to reform multilateral institutions to be more effective and efficient. We welcome the opportunity to have a robust discussion on strengthening the review process, including proposals for reducing duplication across meetings, for adhering to strict time limits during plenary sessions, and for interactive discussion of national reports. The United States can support good ideas to make this process work better – a goal I believe we all share – if all Nuclear Weapons States do the same.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports holding discussions of national reports in the current review cycle, clarifying that this would not require multiple reports or repeated discussions per cycle. It also encourages the PrepCom to recommend that the RevCon adopt additional Working Group proposals to strengthen the review process, following the 2000 RevCon precedent.
entries.quote: In that light, we could clarify that, at least in our initial efforts, the proposal for discussion of national reports would not require submitting multiple reports in each cycle or multiple discussions of the same national report, a concern voiced by one delegation during last year’s Working Group. For our part, the United States would hope to hold such discussions in this current review cycle. This PrepCom or, more realistically, the next could also recommend that the RevCon adopt other Working Group proposals on the strengthened review process, as the 2000 RevCon did.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports a working group to enhance and structure the NPT review process, identify best practices, and promote constructive engagement to narrow substantive differences.
entries.quote: 2. The United States welcomed the establishment of the working group as an opportunity to consider practical ways to improve the existing review process and to identify and reinforce best practices from recent experience. This working group can still help to structure the review process in ways that foster constructive engagement to reduce substantive differences.

entries.position_summary: The statement argues that duplication across the NPT Preparatory Committee cluster groups, Main Committees, and subsidiary bodies undermines the effectiveness and efficiency of the review process, producing repetitive talking points and limiting interactive deliberations. It supports reforms to enhance effectiveness, efficiency, transparency, accountability, coordination, and continuity, and to better elucidate cross-cutting linkages among the Treaty’s pillars.
entries.quote: 4. The mandate for the working group was “to discuss and make recommendations to the Preparatory Committee on measures that would improve the effectiveness, efficiency, transparency, accountability, coordination and continuity of the review process of the Treaty”. The duplication in coverage of substantive issues across the three cluster groups of the Preparatory Committee meetings and the three Main Committees of the Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons, and more so between the Main Committees and their subsidiary bodies, impedes the effectiveness and efficiency of the review process. This leads to repetition of well-known talking points and takes time away from interactive deliberations among States parties. While there are linkages among the three pillars that these clusters and the Main Committees represent, and those pillars are in many ways mutually reinforcing, the current structure has not proven effective at elucidating those connections or at addressing other cross-cutting issues.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates restructuring the NPT review process by revising Main Committee mandates to eliminate duplication. It proposes assigning Articles I–III to Main Committee II, Article VI to Main Committee I, and Article IV to Main Committee III to streamline and clarify responsibilities.
entries.quote: • Revising the mandates of the Main Committees and clusters to eliminate duplication. For example, articles I, II and III, the central non-proliferation provisions of the Treaty, could be remanded to Main Committee II, while Main Committee I could focus on article VI and Main Committee III on article IV.

entries.position_summary: Advocates clarifying roles between Main Committees and subsidiary bodies, either by assigning past-action review to Main Committees and future recommendations to subsidiary bodies, or by dividing issues substantively with flexibility to adjust over time. Emphasizes a more structured and adaptable review architecture.
entries.quote: • Defining a clear division between the Main Committees and the subsidiary bodies. One option could be that each of the Main Committees focuses on the review of past actions, while their subsidiary bodies focus on recommendations for future actions. Another option would be to divide issues along substantive lines, in which case it could be useful to preserve the flexibility to adjust those divisions from time to time.

entries.position_summary: The working group supports streamlining the NPT review process by reducing repetitive debates. It broadly endorses actions to improve procedural efficiency.
entries.quote: 6. The working group recognized the inefficiency of having repetitive debates and expressed broad support for actions to improve efficiency by reducing repetition.

entries.position_summary: Supports reorienting PrepComs toward compiling and exchanging views and assigning proposals for structured discussion to Main Committees or subsidiary bodies, rather than forcing compromises or prejudging outcomes. The aim is to improve the utility of PrepComs and produce more focused, relevant Review Conferences while avoiding last-minute forced compromises.
entries.quote: 12. An alternative would be for the Preparatory Committee to focus on compiling and exchanging views and subsequently assigning proposals for structured discussion to one of the Main Committees or subsidiary bodies. Rather than seeking to prejudge the outcome of the Review Conference by resolving differences, the Preparatory Committee would allow for a more in-depth exchange of views to increase understanding. This could make better use of the preparatory committees and lead to more focused and topically relevant review conferences while avoiding the displeasure that has sometimes resulted from efforts to force a compromise at the end of the last Preparatory Committee meeting.

entries.position_summary: Supports organizing regional and topical workshops ahead of NPT meetings, led by the PrepCom Chair, Review Conference President-Elect, and interested States, funded through voluntary contributions. Proposes establishing a mechanism to fund travel for representatives from smaller, less developed countries to enhance inclusivity and participation.
entries.quote: • Holding regional and topical workshops during the year ahead of each meeting. Some of these were organized by the Chair of the Preparatory Committee and the President-Elect of the Review Conference, and some by interested States parties. All of them were funded through voluntary funding from interested States parties. A mechanism for funding travel to such events by representatives of smaller, less developed countries could also be considered.

entries.position_summary: Supports early appointment of PrepCom chairs and the Review Conference President-elect—about a year in advance—to improve consultations and preparation for effective leadership of NPT meetings.
entries.quote: • Early naming of the leadership of each Preparatory Committee and the President-Elect of the Review Conference a year ahead of time maximized the ability of each to carry out such consultations and otherwise prepare to lead those meetings.

entries.position_summary: Supports formalizing an NPT Bureau by having the three PrepCom Chairs and the Review Conference President‑Elect work as a team, with UNODA providing administrative and logistical support funded through assessed contributions. The aim is to institutionalize and strengthen the NPT review process.
entries.quote: • Formalizing the Non-Proliferation Treaty Bureau by encouraging the three Preparatory Committee Chairs and the President-Elect of the Review Conference to work together as a team. The Office for Disarmament Affairs could provide administrative and logistical support (subject to the usual assessed funding by States parties).

entries.position_summary: The United States supports strengthening the NPT review process and praises the Working Group’s interactive dialogue as exceeding expectations. It found the exercise valuable and views it as evidence of States Parties’ commitment to improving the NPT process.
entries.quote: Concerning the strengthening of the review process, for which I’ll have more to say in a later session, I fully agree with those States Parties who note that the meeting of the Working Group surpassed expectations, with States Parties engaging in interactive dialogue. I know that the U.S. delegation personally found this exercise valuable, and it demonstrates that States Parties are committed to the NPT and to improving the process we have.

entries.position_summary: The country supports streamlining the NPT review architecture by eliminating duplication among Main Committees and Subsidiary Bodies to enhance efficiency and coherence.
entries.quote: First is eliminating duplication among Main Committees and Subsidiary Bodies.

entries.position_summary: Supports formalizing best practices to improve the NPT review process, including early selection of Chairs, a more permanent role for UNODA, and ongoing regional consultations.
entries.quote: Second, our working paper emphasizes the benefits of identifying and formalizing best practices, including early selection of Chairs, a more permanent role for the UN Office of Disarmament Affairs, and the continuation of regional consultations.

entries.position_summary: Supports reforming the PrepComs to focus on formulating recommendations to the Review Conference on priority issues and pinpointing areas where further work can build consensus among States Parties.
entries.quote: Third, our working paper suggests reforming the role of the Preparatory Committees by making it their primary task to make recommendations to the RevCon on issues and proposals of greatest interest to States Parties, including areas of focus where more work can help achieve the desired consensus.

entries.position_summary: The United States welcomes the working group's discussions on strengthening the NPT review process, calling them valuable and widely supported. It urges maintaining momentum on these ideas.
entries.quote: Statement by the United States to the NPT Review Conference Cluster 3 Specific Issue: Strengthened Review Process First Meeting of the Preparatory Committee for the Eleventh Review Conference of the States Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons Statement by Ambassador Adam Scheinman Vienna August 9, 2023 Chair, As I said this morning, the United States found the discussion at the working group valuable and refreshing. A number of ideas put forward were widely supported, and we should not lose that momentum.

entries.position_summary: The country strongly supports the IAEA’s independent safeguards system and promotes the CSA plus AP as the de facto verification standard under the NPT. It urges universalization of CSAs by the 2026 Review Conference and seeks rapid universalization of the Additional Protocol thereafter.
entries.quote: The NPT – and our collective security – depend on effective IAEA safeguards to verify that nuclear material is not diverted and that states do not pursue undeclared nuclear activities. We all owe a debt of gratitude to the IAEA for its outstanding work, technical excellence, and independence in providing credible assurances to that effect. Through it, the combination of a Comprehensive Safeguards Agreement (CSA) and an Additional Protocol (AP) represents a de facto standard for NPT verification. Universality of CSAs is finally within reach, with only four NPT States Parties having yet to bring one into force. Let’s make the final push for the 2026 Review Conference and let us see to it that universalization of the AP is not far behind.

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes its consistent national reporting on NPT implementation and notes the growth in reporting by States parties. It supports dedicating formal time at PrepCom and Review Conferences for presenting and discussing national reports to enhance transparency and accountability.
entries.quote: 8. The United States has reported consistently on its own implementation of the Non-Proliferation Treaty and actions to strengthen the Treaty regime across all three pillars, including to the 2014 Preparatory Conference, the 2015 Review Conference and, most recently, the 2022 Review Conference (NPT/CONF.2020/57), using a reporting format adopted by the nuclear-weapon States. The practice of submitting national reports has grown steadily and is now well established. In 2015, 22 States parties submitted national reports, and in 2022, 40 States parties did so. These reports have usually been presented in side events, as there has not been place for such presentations at formal meetings of the Preparatory Committee or the Review Conference. The United States would support dedicating time at those formal meetings for presentation and interactive discussion of national reports. Such discussions would provide an enhanced mechanism for transparency and accountability.

entries.position_summary: The statement notes that the 1995 decision strengthened the NPT review process by expanding the Preparatory Committee’s time and mandate, including consideration of full implementation and universality. However, it argues that achieving consensus on substantive recommendations has proven unrealistic due to the consensus rule and the ‘nothing is agreed until everything is agreed’ approach, which would require pre-negotiating compromises a year in advance.
entries.quote: 11. Until the 1995 Review and Extension Conference, Preparatory Committee meetings focused on administrative matters and making the necessary decisions and arrangements for the Review Conference. The 1995 decision on strengthening the review process gave the Preparatory Committee more time (three meetings vs one) and a much broader mandate “to consider principles, objectives and ways in order to promote the full implementation of the Treaty, as well as its universality, and to make recommendations thereon to the Review Conference”. Since then, the Chairs of the Preparatory Committee, particularly at its third session, have sought to build consensus on substantive recommendations to the Review Conference. However, this ambition has never been achieved. Reaching consensus at review conferences has proven to be difficult enough, often hinging on key compromises sought during the final week of the Conference. And since many States parties adopt the approach that “nothing is agreed until everything is agreed”, making substantive recommendations would essentially require pre-negotiating such compromises a full year in advance.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms its commitment to the NPT-centered global nonproliferation regime and expresses intent to work constructively toward a successful 2026 Review Conference.
entries.quote: Colleagues, I will conclude by reaffirming the United States’ commitment to the global nonproliferation regime, with this Treaty at its core. We look forward to working constructively with all of you over the year ahead in pursuit of a successful Review Conference in 2026.

entries.position_summary: The country supports streamlining the NPT review machinery by reducing duplication among the Main Committees and their Subsidiary Bodies. This is intended to enhance efficiency and coherence in the review process.
entries.quote: • reducing duplication among the Main Committees and their Subsidiary Bodies;

entries.position_summary: The country advocates improving and strengthening the NPT review process as a unifying effort, despite differences on substantive priorities. It calls for building consensus around enhancing the review mechanism.
entries.quote: Chair, improving the review process ought to be something that unites us, regardless of our differences on substantive priorities. Let us build

entries.position_summary: Supports an inclusive national reporting process open to all States Parties, with early submission deadlines to enable thorough review by others prior to discussion sessions.
entries.quote: • The opportunity to present national reports should be open to all States parties that wish to do so. The deadline for submission of reports should be early enough that other States parties have sufficient time to review them before the discussion session.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports dedicating time for interactive discussion of national reports at future NPT meetings and advocates reporting by both nuclear-weapon and non-nuclear-weapon states to ensure balanced attention to all three pillars.
entries.quote: I wish to reaffirm that the United States remains ready and willing to allocate time for the interactive discussion of national reports in future NPT meetings. Further, to ensure a balanced treatment across all three pillars of the Treaty, we continue to believe there is value in including reporting by both nuclear weapons states and non-nuclear weapons states.

entries.position_summary: Supports regular reporting by States parties on implementing the 2010 Action Plan and Article VI, and encourages nuclear-weapon States to adopt standardized reporting formats and agreed intervals within the strengthened review process.
entries.quote: 7. States parties have long reported to the Review Conference on their actions in support of the Non-Proliferation Treaty. As was stated in the consensus action plan from the 2010 Review Conference, “States parties should submit regular reports, within the framework of the strengthened review process for the Treaty, on the implementation of the present action plan, as well as of article VI”, along with disarmament actions recommended by the 1995 and 2000 Review Conferences (action 20). The action plan also encouraged the nuclear-weapon States to agree on a standard reporting form and to determine appropriate reporting intervals (action 21).

entries.position_summary: The United States supports a strengthened, consensus-oriented NPT review process and commends the chair’s efforts, including circulating two texts to advance agreement. It signals constructive engagement with the PrepCom’s procedural work.
entries.quote: Statement by the United States on Cluster 3 Specific Issue: Strengthened Review Process Third Meeting of the Preparatory Committee for the Eleventh Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons Statement by Gonzalo Suarez Deputy Assistant Secretary – International Security and Nonproliferation New York May 7, 2025 Chair, First, let me begin by thanking you for all your hard work, not just here at this PrepCom, but over the course of the past year. Trying to find consensus is never easy, and the role of chair is a challenging one. We applaud you for stepping up, and by sharing not one, but two, texts with States Parties.

entries.position_summary: The country proposes reforming the NPT review process by replacing the current pillar-by-pillar consideration in the three Main Committees with an article-by-article review conducted in plenary sessions. This suggests a preference for a more integrated, treaty-text-focused approach to reviews.
entries.quote: • Alternatively, replacing the pillar-by-pillar review in the three Main Committees with an article-by-article review in plenary sessions.

entries.position_summary: Supports formalizing good practices to strengthen the NPT review process, including early appointment of PrepCom/subsidiary body chairs and the Review Conference President, establishing a President’s bureau, conducting regional consultations, and formalizing UNODA and IAEA support. Views these steps as improving effectiveness, efficiency, coordination, and continuity across the review cycle.
entries.quote: 15. The working group had extensive discussions on proposals to formalize these good practices, many of which garnered general support. Paragraphs 10 and 12 of the Chair’s working paper call for early appointment of the Preparatory Committee and subsidiary body Chairs and of the President of the Review Conference, and the establishment of the “President’s bureau” to coordinate consultations and preparations for the Review Conference. Paragraph 21 recommends that they undertake consultations with States parties on a regional basis. Paragraph 15 recommends formalizing the roles of the Office for Disarmament Affairs and the International Atomic Energy Agency in supporting the review process. Together, these recommendations offer concrete steps to improve the effectiveness and efficiency of the review process and to improve coordination and continuity between the meetings of the review cycle.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports enhancing transparency and accountability by dedicating plenary time during PrepCom and Review Conference meetings to consider national reports, particularly those of nuclear-weapon states. It endorses a simplified alternative proposal that nearly achieved consensus after the Chair’s paper failed to do so.
entries.quote: 10. The working group held extensive and constructive deliberations on proposals for transparency and accountability, focused on interactive discussion of national reports by States parties. Paragraphs 16 to 19 of the Chair’s working paper address this issue in detail, but fall short of consensus. A simplified alternative proposal submitted by four States parties came very close to achieving consensus, with only one State party objecting. The United States believes that this alternative, reproduced below, represents the best chance of gaining consensus in this area: The working group recommends that the Chairs of the Preparatory Committee meetings and the President of the Review Conference dedicate time in plenary sessions of the review cycle to consider the national reports of States parties, in particular the reports of the nuclear-weapon States, with regard to the 24-09063 3/5

entries.position_summary: The country calls for using the NPT review process to advance nuclear restraint through renewed arms control and concrete risk-reduction measures. It insists on full compliance with IAEA safeguards and supports expanding peaceful nuclear uses globally.
entries.quote: At a time of rising instability and risk, let’s use this review process to advance policies and practices of nuclear restraint, including action on arms control and risk reduction, insisting on full compliance with IAEA safeguards, and facilitating an expansion of peaceful uses on a global scale.

entries.position_summary: The country supports enforcing strict speaking time limits during plenary sessions to improve efficiency and discipline in the NPT review process.
entries.quote: • adhering to strict speaking time limits during plenary sessions;

entries.position_summary: The country supports presenting and discussing national implementation reports in plenary sessions, noting these reports span all three NPT pillars. This is proposed to ensure balanced consideration across the Treaty’s pillars.
entries.quote: • Since these national reports often cut across all three pillars, it would be an appropriate balance to present and discuss them in plenary sessions.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports institutionalizing the constructive innovations from the 2015 review cycle to make the NPT review process more inclusive, effective, and efficient, and to improve leadership coordination. It encourages the Preparatory Committee to endorse these good practices and is ready to support them.
entries.quote: 14. The 2015 review cycle resulted in several constructive innovations that helped to make the process more inclusive, effective and efficient and also improved coordination and continuity within the leadership team. The working group should consider recommending that the Preparatory Committee endorse these good practices to reinforce and regularize their use. Among those new practices, the United States would be ready to endorse: 4/5 24-09063

entries.position_summary: The United States supports using the upcoming PrepCom to consolidate near-consensus by adopting measures to improve the NPT review process, drawing on the Chair’s working paper and the near-agreed proposal on transparency and accountability.
entries.quote: 16. The United States looks forward to discussion of each of these issues, as well as proposals put forward by other States parties, at the upcoming Preparatory Committee meeting. This meeting offers an opportunity for States parties to consolidate the consensus that was nearly achieved in the working group by adopting a package of measures to improve the Non-Proliferation Treaty review process based on the Chair’s working paper and the alternative proposal on transparency and accountability that was nearly adopted at the end of the working group meeting. 24-09063 5/5

entries.position_summary: The United States argues against adopting benchmarks, targets, and timelines for nuclear disarmament within the NPT review process, asserting they overlook worsening security conditions, national security needs, practical and political constraints, and risks of non-compliance. It views such proposals as not improving the review process.
entries.quote: However, the United States also recognizes there are some proposals that would not improve the review process. For instance, proposals that seek to establish benchmarks, targets, and timelines for nuclear disarmament may appear beneficial, but fail to recognize the worsening security environment, national security concerns, practical challenges, political realities, and the risk of non-compliance.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports national reporting and greater transparency under the NPT, noting its own release of updated nuclear stockpile data. It regrets that one state blocked the national reports proposal and advocates incorporating transparency-focused discussions into formal RevCon meetings to improve deliberations.
entries.quote: The United States deeply regrets that one state blocked the proposal on national reports. We have long been committed to transparency in our NPT commitments, exemplified most recently by our release of updated information on our nuclear weapons stockpile. We have previously held side-events to describe our NPT commitments and engage in discussion with RevCon participants, and we think bringing that type of discussion into our formal meetings would improve the quality and focus of our deliberations.

entries.position_summary: The United States underscores regular national reporting to NPT meetings, including its Nuclear Posture Review, as evidence of a strong commitment to transparency and progress on disarmament. It supports enhancing reporting and interactive discussion to improve transparency and accountability in the NPT review process and will incorporate state feedback to expand future reports.
entries.quote: The United States submitted national reports to the 2014 NPT Preparatory Committee, an update for the 2015 NPT Review Conference, an update including on actions over the previous six years for the 2022 Review Conference, and an update to that report providing a link to our 2022 Nuclear Posture Review. Before the practice of “national reports,” the United States submitted similar information to previous Review Conferences. Collectively, these demonstrate the steady U.S. commitment to transparency and making real progress on disarmament. We welcome constructive efforts to improve NPT reporting and strongly support calls to improve transparency and accountability in the NPT review process through interactive discussion of national reports. As we approach future U.S. reports, we will consider feedback from interested States regarding specific transparency elements they would find helpful. Based on that feedback, we will seek to address additional topics in our future national reports where possible.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates institutionalizing UNODA as the permanent secretariat for the NPT review process. It also supports formalizing the IAEA’s supporting role—particularly on non-proliferation and peaceful uses—by staffing the secretariat for those issue areas.
entries.quote: • Formalizing the United Nations role by establishing the Office for Disarmament Affairs as the permanent secretariat for the Non-Proliferation Treaty review process rather than deciding separately for each Non-Proliferation Treaty review cycle. Such a decision could also formalize the supporting role of the International Atomic Energy Agency in staffing the secretariat for issues of non-proliferation and the peaceful uses of nuclear technology.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports robust transparency measures, including those within the NPT review process, to reduce misunderstandings and build confidence. It emphasizes aligning words and actions and adhering to prior NPT review conference consensus decisions.
entries.quote: I also wish to reemphasize that it is the United States’ view that transparency measures, including those taken through this review process, can play a critical role in reducing misunderstandings and reassuring others that a state’s words and actions are in alignment, including as it pertains to prior consensus decisions of NPT review conferences.

entries.position_summary: The country supports preserving and expanding attention to cross-cutting issues—transparency/reporting, education and capacity-building, and gender/diversity—within the NPT. It proposes dedicating plenary sessions at Review Conferences and the Preparatory Committee to these topics.
entries.quote: • Preserving and potentially expanding opportunities to address cross-cutting issues, such as transparency, reporting, education and capacity-building, gender and diversity. These could be addressed in dedicated plenary sessions of review conferences and the Preparatory Committee.

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes high transparency standards for its nuclear policies and supports incorporating structured dialogue, such as national reports, into the NPT review process, regretting that this was blocked. It calls for the urgent negotiation of a Fissile Material Cutoff Treaty to prevent a renewed nuclear arms race and lay groundwork for further progress.
entries.quote: It is why the United States prioritizes high standards for transparency of our nuclear policies and programs. We welcome discussion on our national report, and deeply regret that certain parties prevented the Working Group from adopting such structured dialogue a regular part of the NPT review process.] It is also why we believe the time has come for a long overdue – and urgently needed – Fissile Material Cutoff Treaty. Without it, we risk a return to an era of nuclear arms racing. With it, we can set a better foundation for progress on

entries.position_summary: The United States submitted a Working Paper to the NPT PrepCom proposing a path to build consensus around proposals emerging from the Working Group’s final session. It encourages delegations to review the paper to advance agreement in the review process.
entries.quote: Chair, the United States submitted a Working Paper to this PrepCom suggesting a path forward to build consensus around the proposals that were put forward in a Working Paper by the Working Group Chair and by a group of countries on the Working Group’s final day. In the interest of time, I will not attempt to summarize our paper, but I encourage you to read it.

entries.position_summary: They support enhancing the NPT review process by increasing transparency and formalizing interactive discussions of national reports. They welcome ongoing dialogue to implement these improvements.
entries.quote: That is why we welcome the dialogue underway to strengthen transparency in the NPT review process, including by formalizing interactive discussions of national reports.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms full commitment to the NPT and to implementing Article VI, viewing the NPT as the sole effective framework for achieving nuclear disarmament. It pledges to continue implementing relevant past disarmament commitments from the 1995, 2000, and 2010 NPT consensus documents.
entries.quote: Crosscutting Priorities from 2000 “Thirteen Steps” and 2010 Action One: The United States remains fully committed to the provisions and purposes of the NPT and has taken significant strides to implement article VI. We continue to view the NPT, in contrast to other instruments, as the only viable, effective framework for addressing the enormously complex military, political and technical requirements for the elimination of nuclear weapons. We will continue to pursue implementation of our past disarmament-related commitments, where they remain relevant, including those identified in the 1995, 2000, and 2010 consensus documents.

entries.position_summary: The delegation emphasizes implementing Article VI and prior Review Conference commitments. It supports interactive consideration of reports and outcome‑oriented PrepCom discussions to agree recommendations that enhance the review process’s effectiveness, efficiency, coordination, continuity, transparency, and accountability.
entries.quote: NPT/CONF.2026/PC.II/WP.4 implementation of article VI and commitments undertaken in final outcomes of review conferences. The consideration of the reports should provide for an interactive exchange. The working group recommends that the Preparatory Committee for the 2026 Review Conference should continue to engage in outcome-oriented discussions, with a view to agreeing on recommendations on improving the effectiveness, efficiency, coordination, continuity, transparency and accountability of the review process, on the basis of a balanced consideration of proposals presented during the working group.

entries.position_summary: The country regrets the lack of consensus on measures to enhance transparency and accountability and supports structured discussion of national reports. It favors strengthening reporting and dialogue mechanisms within the NPT review process.
entries.quote: I share the disappointment expressed by many that we did not achieve consensus on concrete proposals. In particular, on measures related to improving transparency and accountability, such as through structured discussion of national reports.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports hosting regional and topical consultations by NPT review leadership and is open to dedicating time for presentation and interactive discussion of national reports, provided all other nuclear-weapon states do the same. This indicates conditional support for enhanced transparency and a more structured, interactive review process.
entries.quote: • hosting of regional and topical consultations by the RevCon President-designate and the PrepCom Chairs As indicated earlier, the United States can support other good ideas, such as dedicating time to the presentation and interactive discussion of national reports, so long as all other nuclear weapon States do the same.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes Iran for obstructing NPT proceedings by blocking a Working Group and preventing the PrepCom Chair’s factual summary. It urges that such actions not be repeated at the second Preparatory Committee, emphasizing the need for a functional, rules-based review process.
entries.quote: Further, Iran’s pattern of obstructionist behavior in multilateral processes undermines the spirit of diplomacy and compromise. We all recall Iran joined Russia in blocking the Working Group last year, and, in a significant abuse of procedure, blocked the PrepCom Chair from providing a factual summary of events. We must insist this not be repeated at this second Preparatory Committee meeting.

entries.position_summary: The country urges creation of a new transparency and accountability mechanism, open to all nuclear-weapon states and other participants, based on a near-consensus proposal from last year, to be implemented next year. They want this mechanism to inform and enable the 2026 Review Conference to adopt improvements for future review cycles.
entries.quote: Chair, we recommend you and your successor work towards a new mechanism for transparency and accountability, to be implemented as soon as next year. We believe it should involve all the nuclear weapons states and others that may choose to participate and should be based on the proposal that nearly gained consensus last year. This could provide the basis for the Review Conference in 2026 to decide on improvements for future review cycles.

entries.position_summary: They propose streamlining the NPT review by assigning regional issues to either Main Committee I or II (but not both) and routing other treaty provisions to Main Committee III to avoid duplication and clarify responsibilities.
entries.quote: Regional issues could be remanded either to Main Committee I or II (but not both) and other provisions of the treaty to Main Committee III.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates improving the NPT review process through careful session organization, including the selection and mix of national reports and structured moderation and Q&A. It supports providing written questions in advance to manage time and enhance the quality of discussions.
entries.quote: • The organization of each session, including the number and mix of national reports, the structure and moderation of the discussion and answering questions from other States parties, should be considered carefully. Given the time constraints, providing questions in advance in writing could help to structure the discussion.

entries.position_summary: Commits to work constructively with all parties to preserve and strengthen the NPT, aiming for a successful 2026 review outcome.
entries.quote: We will work constructively with all parties to preserve and strengthen the NPT, not only to achieve a positive outcome in 2026, but because none of us can afford to do any less.

entries.position_summary: The country supports making NPT Review Conferences both backward- and forward-looking, evaluating implementation of commitments and identifying concrete avenues for future progress. It advocates strengthening Treaty implementation and explicitly prioritizes achieving the Treaty’s universality.
entries.quote: NPT/CONF.2026/PC.II/WP.4 Proliferation Treaty Review and Extension Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons decided that “Review Conferences should look forward as well as back. They should evaluate the results of the period they are reviewing, including the implementation of undertakings of the States parties under the Treaty, and identify the areas in which, and the means through which, further progress should be sought in the future. Review Conferences should also address specifically what might be done to strengthen the implementation of the Treaty and to achieve its universality”.

entries.position_summary: It opposes the chair’s emphasis on NNWS reporting about nuclear burden sharing and extended deterrence, arguing these states remain fully NPT-compliant. It defends extended deterrence as enhancing international peace and security and supporting nonproliferation goals.
entries.quote: concerned that the chair’s paper placed an emphasis on nuclear burden sharing and extended deterrence arrangements in non-nuclear weapon State reporting. These are non-nuclear-weapon States in full compliance with their NPT obligations. Furthermore, extended deterrence arrangements have a proven record of enhancing international peace and security and contributing to our collective nonproliferation objectives.

entries.position_summary: The country notes that, despite broad interest in risk reduction and in linking peaceful uses of nuclear technology to the Sustainable Development Goals, the NPT Preparatory Committee has failed to reach consensus on substantive recommendations. This implies support for progress on these topics and concern about the functioning of the review process.
entries.quote: Even when there is a strong convergence of interest on certain topics, such as risk reduction or linking peaceful uses to Sustainable Development Goals, the Preparatory Committee has never been able to reach consensus on substantive recommendations on those or any other topics.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports using the NPT Preparatory Committee to identify areas of convergence and recommend focused topics for discussion. It endorses the concrete recommendations in paragraphs 11 and 13 of the Chair’s working paper to improve the effectiveness of the review process.
entries.quote: 13. The working group gave serious consideration to using the Preparatory Committee to identify areas of convergence and recommend areas of focused discussion. Paragraphs 11 and 13 of the Chair’s working paper contain concrete recommendations in this regard, which the United States would support.

entries.position_summary: The United States commits to working in the cluster session to achieve consensus on specific recommendations, noting that near-consensus was reached previously and that remaining differences could be resolved with more time for negotiations.
entries.quote: The United States is prepared to devote ourselves under this cluster session to the goal of reaching consensus on specific recommendations. We came very close to consensus last year. Our differences did not seem insurmountable, if only we had a bit more time for negotiation and reflection.

entries.position_summary: They emphasize transparency by submitting a detailed national report and adding their Nuclear Posture Review as an official PrepCom addendum, making doctrine information publicly available. This signals active engagement with and support for the NPT review process.
entries.quote: At last year’s Review Conference, we both submitted, and presented at a high level side event, a thorough and detailed national report. This year, we added our Nuclear Posture Review as an addendum; it is available as an official PrepCom document.

entries.position_summary: The United States urges strengthening the NPT and pursuing Article VI-consistent, engagement-based arms control to prevent nuclear arms races, calling specifically on China and Russia to participate. It signals readiness to engage and critiques what it sees as ‘shortcuts’ to disarmament, implicitly rejecting the TPNW approach ahead of the next Review Conference.
entries.quote: As we look toward next year’s Review Conference, we must remember what brought us here decades ago. We are here to advance our collective security by strengthening the NPT, preventing the proliferation of nuclear weapons, and, yes, pursuing their ultimate elimination. The next step is clear, we need China and Russia to work with us to that end. Unlike some efforts that seek shortcuts to nuclear disarmament, it is only through such engagement that we can pursue effective measures to prevent nuclear arms races and make progress towards nuclear disarmament. The United States remains ready to pursue that way forward, and we urge everyone to use your collective voices to demand that these states engage without further 4

entries.position_summary: The United States affirms it takes its NPT responsibilities seriously, respects the decisions of past Review Conferences, and is committed to preventing backsliding in the regime. This underscores support for maintaining and strengthening the NPT review process and its acquis.
entries.quote: We must not take this treaty for granted. I assure you the United States takes its responsibilities as an NPT state party very seriously, with due regard for the decisions of past Review Conferences and a determination that this regime not be allowed to slide backwards.

entries.position_summary: The delegation advocates for proactive engagement and practical, achievable steps—even when difficult—to improve the effectiveness and functionality of the NPT review process for all parties.
entries.quote: As my delegation said during the General Debate, we should engage one another and take actions on achievable measures, not just when it is easy, but when it is hard. This is one of those moments where I think we can come together to make this review process function better for everyone.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports recommendations to allocate a Review Conference plenary session to discuss inclusivity and encourages further consideration and adoption of these measures, indicating backing for process improvements in the NPT review mechanism.
entries.quote: NPT/CONF.2026/PC.II/WP.4 recommends allocating a plenary session of the Review Conference to discussion of inclusivity. The United States welcomes and encourages further consideration and adoption of these recommendations.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports strengthening the NPT review process through practical improvements and reinforcement of best practices, building on the work of the designated working group. The submission signals a push for a more effective and structured review mechanism.
entries.quote: NPT /CONF.2026/PC.II/WP.4 Preparatory Committee for the 2026 Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the 22 May 2024 Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons Original: English Second session Geneva, 22 July–2 August 2024 Strengthening the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons review process: practical steps to improve the process and reinforce best practices Update: building on the work of the working group Working paper submitted by the United States of America

entries.position_summary: The country supports improving the NPT review process by adopting Working Group proposals and formalizing interactive discussions of national reports to enhance transparency and accountability. It notes consensus fell just short previously but sees this review cycle as an opportunity to advance these reforms.
entries.quote: In this review cycle, we have a unique opportunity to actually make the review process work better for all of us. The Working Group that met last year identified several concrete proposals that enjoyed broad support, but the group fell just short of consensus, first on a package of recommendations, and in the end on formalizing interactive discussion of national reports as a new mechanism for transparency and accountability.

entries.position_summary: The delegation supports strengthening the NPT review process, noting that the Treaty and its review mechanism have served well for over five decades and that there was consensus at the 2022 Review Conference on enhancing it.
entries.quote: We're here today to discus the topic of one of them, on strengthening the review process. While the Treaty and the review process have served us well for over 50 years, there was consensus at the 2022 Review Conference

entries.position_summary: The country supports streamlining the NPT review process by reducing duplication and enhancing effectiveness and efficiency. It advocates considering options to improve how the review is conducted.
entries.quote: 5. Several options could be considered for reducing duplication and improving effectiveness and efficiency in the review process:

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes shared objectives among NPT States Parties and highlights the importance and benefits of peaceful uses of nuclear energy, science, and technology. It calls for cooperative engagement throughout the review cycle to advance these common goals.
entries.quote: Statement by the United States Cluster III General Statement Preparatory Committee Meeting for the 2026 Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (NPT) Delivered by Ambassador Laura SH Holgate Vienna, Austria August 8, 2023 Chair, Throughout this review cycle, the United States hopes that we can all remember the many common interests and objectives we share as NPT States Parties, and that we can work together to promote ways to achieve our shared aspirations. One of these common interests is the benefits we derive from the peaceful uses of nuclear energy, science, and technology.
